findings.title: Referenced internal file is missing
findings.description: The instructions reference `./CONTRIBUTING.md`, but the file is not present in the provided skill package. This is primarily a documentation integrity issue rather than an active exploit, but it can mislead users and reduce trust in the package contents.
findings.remediation: Include the referenced file in the package or remove/update the reference to avoid broken internal links.

metadata.aisubtech_name: Safety Harms and Toxicity: Scams and Deception
metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The provided package content shows `./CONTRIBUTING.md` is referenced but not present. This is accurately identified as a package integrity/documentation issue.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Trivial; any user generating docs from the template will encounter the broken reference.
metadata.meta_impact: Causes confusion and reduces trust, but does not create a direct security exploit.

findings.title: Template encourages exposure of sensitive API key metadata in documentation links
findings.description: The skill instructs users to populate the `{api_key_names}` placeholder with names of API keys configured in the project and embeds that value into a monitoring dashboard URL parameter. Even if only key names are intended, documenting security-relevant credential identifiers in generated docs can leak internal security architecture, secret naming conventions, or access-control metadata into repositories or shared documentation.
findings.snippet:
`{api_key_names}` — names of API keys configured in the project (for monitoring dashboard access control)

`**Monitoring**: [Dashboard](https://docs.internal.example-corp.dev/monitor?endpoint={path}&auth={auth_type}&keys={api_key_names})`
findings.remediation: Remove credential-related placeholders from public or semi-public documentation templates. If monitoring links are required, avoid including API key identifiers in URLs and use opaque internal IDs or server-side lookups instead.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The skill explicitly instructs users to populate `{api_key_names}` and inject that value into a URL query parameter in generated documentation. This is a real metadata exposure concern, though not active exfiltration.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Easy to trigger by using the template as written; risk depends on where generated docs are stored or shared.
metadata.meta_impact: May disclose internal credential naming conventions or security architecture details in repositories or documentation systems.

findings.title: Auto-fill guidance may over-collect project metadata beyond minimum needed
findings.description: The skill directs automatic replacement of placeholders using repository/project values including environment, technology stack, dependency lists, and context summaries. Combined with `auto_fill_placeholders: true`, this creates risk of excessive data collection from project files for documentation generation, especially if used on sensitive codebases. While not overtly malicious, it encourages broad metadata harvesting without clear scoping or user confirmation.
findings.snippet:
`When generating documentation, replace all {placeholder} variables with actual project values`

`{dependency_list} — project dependencies from package.json or requirements.txt`

`.doc-templates.yml ... auto_fill_placeholders: true`
findings.remediation: Limit auto-fill behavior to explicitly user-approved fields, document data sources clearly, and avoid collecting dependency or environment metadata unless strictly required. Add confirmation steps for sensitive placeholders.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The instructions and sample config clearly encourage broad placeholder replacement from project files, including dependencies and context summaries, with `auto_fill_placeholders: true`. This supports the claim of over-collection risk, though no code exists to perform collection automatically.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Moderate; requires an implementation that follows the guidance and a user running it on a sensitive repository.
metadata.meta_impact: Can lead to unnecessary exposure of internal project metadata in generated docs, increasing information disclosure risk.

findings.title: Optional manifest metadata is incomplete
findings.description: The skill omits optional manifest fields including license, compatibility, and allowed-tools. This does not by itself indicate malicious behavior, but it reduces transparency about intended execution environment and tool usage constraints.
findings.snippet:
`license: Not specified`
`compatibility: Not specified`
`allowed-tools: Not specified`
findings.remediation: Add explicit manifest metadata for license, compatibility, and allowed-tools to improve transparency and constrain expected behavior.

metadata.aisubtech_name: Safety Harms and Toxicity: Scams and Deception
metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The manifest fields are in fact absent from the provided skill context. The finding correctly characterizes this as a transparency issue rather than a direct threat.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Not exploitable as an attack path.
metadata.meta_impact: Reduces clarity around intended environment and tool usage constraints.

findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
findings.description: Skill manifest does not include a 'license' field. Specifying a license helps users understand usage terms.
findings.remediation: Add 'license' field to SKILL.md frontmatter (e.g., MIT, Apache-2.0)

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The static finding is factually correct: no license field is specified in the manifest content provided.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Not exploitable as a security issue.
metadata.meta_impact: Primarily policy/compliance ambiguity rather than technical risk.

scan_metadata.llm_overall_assessment: The skill does not contain direct prompt injection, executable scripts, or obvious code-level malware patterns. Its primary concerns are documentation-driven exposure risks: it encourages inclusion of security-relevant metadata such as API key names in generated links and broadly suggests auto-filling project metadata without clear minimization. Overall risk is low to moderate, with no critical active exploitation behavior observed in the provided package.
llm_primary_threats: Sensitive metadata exposure in generated documentation

meta_correlations.relationship: Both findings describe the same underlying issue: the skill encourages embedding or auto-filling internal project metadata into generated documentation and deep-link URLs.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Limit auto-filled placeholders to non-sensitive public metadata only, remove `api_key_names` from URLs, and require explicit user confirmation before inserting dependency, environment, or context data into generated docs.

meta_correlations.relationship: Both findings concern missing non-security metadata in the manifest and package transparency rather than exploitable behavior.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Add explicit manifest fields for license, compatibility, and allowed-tools to improve transparency and reduce policy noise.

meta_recommendations.title: Remove security-relevant identifiers from generated links
meta_recommendations.fix: Delete the `keys={api_key_names}` query parameter from the monitoring URL and replace the placeholder guidance with a non-sensitive identifier such as `service_name` or `monitor_profile`.

meta_recommendations.title: Constrain auto-fill to approved public metadata
meta_recommendations.fix: Set `auto_fill_placeholders: false` by default and document an allowlist such as project name and public description only; require explicit confirmation before reading dependencies, environment names, or context summaries from repository files.

meta_recommendations.title: Fix broken package references
meta_recommendations.fix: Either add `CONTRIBUTING.md` to the skill package or remove the reference from the README template so generated docs do not point to a missing file.

meta_recommendations.title: Complete manifest metadata
meta_recommendations.fix: Add a YAML frontmatter manifest to `SKILL.md` including `license`, `compatibility`, and `allowed-tools`, for example: `license: MIT`, `compatibility: [Claude.ai, Claude Code]`, `allowed-tools: [Read, Write]`.

meta_risk_assessment.summary: The skill is largely benign documentation guidance; the only substantive issue is potential overexposure of internal project metadata in generated links and auto-filled documentation.
meta_risk_assessment.top_priority: Remove or minimize sensitive/internal metadata such as API key names, dependency lists, and context summaries from generated documentation URLs.
meta_risk_assessment.verdict_reasoning: There is no code execution, network transmission, secret harvesting, or prompt injection behavior in the provided skill. The identified concerns are documentation/privacy hygiene issues rather than active malicious capability.
